news_summary: Today’s positive human rights news highlights growing support for refugees, increased civic engagement initiatives, improved protections for online safety, and fair compensation for consumers facing economic challenges.
news_summary_fr: Les bonnes nouvelles d'aujourd'hui en matière de droits de l'homme mettent en avant un soutien croissant aux réfugiés, une multiplication des initiatives d'engagement citoyen, un renforcement des mesures de protection de la sécurité en ligne et une indemnisation équitable pour les consommateurs confrontés à des difficultés économiques.
news_summary_es: Las noticias positivas de hoy en materia de derechos humanos ponen de relieve el creciente apoyo a los refugiados, el aumento de las iniciativas de participación ciudadana, la mejora de las medidas de protección de la seguridad en Internet y la indemnización justa para los consumidores que se enfrentan a dificultades económicas.

articles.title: Press release - Better enforcement of rules needed for a safer social media and online environment
articles.summary: Companies should guarantee that the use of social media and the digital environment is safe, particularly for children and young people, MEPs say.Committee on Culture and Education Source : © European Union, 2026 - EP
articles.content:
To protect minors from the impact of social media, MEPs want EU legislation better enforced and implemented. In a report adopted on Tuesday by the Committee on Culture and Education with 17 votes to three and four abstentions, they say that the online environment must be governed by the principles of privacy-by-design and safety-by-default, age-appropriate design, and algorithmic transparency.

Online platforms are responsible for protecting minors

Pointing to the impact of addictive and persuasive design features on wellbeing of children and young people, MEPs want a ban on the most harmful addictive practices and urge platforms to include risk-based safeguards for their recommender systems. They are encouraging the Commission to introduce personal liability in case of serious and persistent non-compliance with the provisions concerning the protection of minors.

The text also highlights the need for increased transparency around social media algorithmic systems, as opaque algorithms and content moderation hampers the ability of young users to understand why certain content is recommended, suppressed or removed, intentionally undermining their capacity to navigate online content.

MEPs also support the introduction of a “youth mode” disabling targeted advertising and limiting addictive design features for minors.

Regulating influencers and preventing digital overexposure of children

Noting the “increasingly significant role in shaping the perceptions, aspirations and social attitudes of children and young people” played by influencers, MEPs want the Commission and EU countries to develop an EU code of conduct for this activity and for a harmonised definition of “influencer marketing”.

Minors should be protected from commercial exploitation activities such as kidfluencing, when children are turned into influencers, and sharenting, when parents document their children’s lives online.

Consistent EU legislation

The Commission must, MEPs argue, ensure consistency among all key EU legal instruments, including the Audiovisual Media Services Directive (AVMSD), the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), the Digital Services Act (DSA), the AI Act, and Digital Fairness Act.

MEPs also put forward proposals for

mandatory ethical standards for 'AI companions' that simulate virtual friendships to prevent the manipulation of a minors’ emotional vulnerability;

improved transparency for the training of AI models, including clear information on the datasets and materials used for AI systems, and child safeguarding measures;

online platforms to mitigate the proliferation of AI-generated content impersonating media professionals and brands, so-called “celebrity scams”, and for the Commission to classify these as a systematic risk and intensify ongoing investigations under the DSA;

online platforms to ensure recommender systems and content moderation mechanisms give more visibility to reliable, and age-appropriate, content and services provided by professional media organisations;

the explicit ban of AI systems generating, manipulating or altering realistic images or videos depicting sexual content of an identifiable person without consent and synthetic child sexual abuse material (CSAM);

systematic monitoring across EU member states, including the development of comparable indicators, on children’s digital habits, access to education and the effectiveness of protection measures, including the implementation of age restrictions, age verification, and safety settings.

Quote

“The responsibility for the safety of children and adolescents must first and foremost lie with those who design and manage digital platforms,” said rapporteur Sandro Ruotolo (S&D, IT), ahead of the vote. “The example used by Commission President Ursula von der Leyen is appropriate: we do not ask children to design seat belts and we do not ask parents to install airbags. Manufacturers have a duty to ensure the safety of their products. The same principle must apply to digital platforms,” he said. “As rapporteur for the European Parliament, I fully agree with this approach. Platforms must eliminate features that encourage addiction, manipulation, and exposure to harmful content or contacts. Safety shall be incorporated into services by design”.

Press conference

After the vote, the rapporteur will take part in a press conference, at 10.30 CEST, at the Anna Politkovskaya press conference room (SPAAK 0A50) and online. Journalists who want to ask questions should join via Interactio.

Next steps

The own-initiative report will be put to vote by Parliament as a whole during the 14-17 September plenary session.

Background

According to Eurostat, on average 97% of young people in the EU used the internet daily in 2024, with global screen time ranging from an average of nine hours daily for 11-14-year groups to six hours per day for children aged 8-10 years.

Over 80% of young people in Europe use social media daily, with time spend online by children and young people aged 9-16 averaging three hours per day. This means that time spend on social networks more than doubled since 2010, according to the EU’s Joint Research Centre. Several studies report on the negative impact of excessive screen time and social media on young people’s mental health.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on meaningful legislative efforts by the European Parliament to enhance the safety and wellbeing of children and young people in the digital environment. It highlights concrete proposals to regulate social media platforms, protect minors from addictive and harmful content, enforce transparency, and prevent exploitation. The scope is broad, affecting millions of young internet users across the EU, and the substance is detailed with specific measures and rationale, making it an inspiring positive development for digital rights and child protection.
articles.personality_title: European Parliament pushes for stronger rules to protect children on social media
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Millions of children and young people in the European Union spend several hours daily on social media and the internet. Studies have shown that too much time online, especially on social media, can harm their mental health and wellbeing.

**What happened** – On Tuesday, the European Parliament’s Committee on Culture and Education voted to support better enforcement of rules that protect minors online. They want social media platforms to design their services with safety in mind from the start. This includes banning harmful addictive features, increasing transparency about how algorithms suggest content, and introducing a “youth mode” that limits advertising and addictive designs for children.

**Impact** – This effort is important because it holds social media companies responsible for protecting young users. For example, the report calls for clear rules against turning children into influencers for commercial gain and wants a code of conduct for influencer marketing. It also addresses new challenges like AI-generated fake content and emotional manipulation by virtual AI companions. These measures aim to make the online world safer and easier for children to understand.

**What's next step** – The report will be voted on by the full European Parliament in September. If approved, it could lead to stronger laws and better protection tools for children across all EU countries. The European Commission and member states will also work on consistent rules covering data privacy, online services, and AI use.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The European Parliament is advancing clear rules to make social media safer and fairer for children by limiting harmful content, increasing transparency, and protecting minors from exploitation.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le Parlement européen renforce les règles pour protéger les enfants sur les réseaux sociaux
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Des millions d’enfants et de jeunes dans l’Union européenne passent plusieurs heures par jour sur les réseaux sociaux et internet. Des études montrent que trop de temps en ligne, surtout sur les réseaux sociaux, peut nuire à leur santé mentale et à leur bien-être.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Mardi, la Commission de la culture et de l’éducation du Parlement européen a voté en faveur d’une meilleure application des règles protégeant les mineurs en ligne. Elle souhaite que les plateformes sociales conçoivent leurs services en intégrant la sécurité dès le départ. Cela inclut l’interdiction des fonctionnalités addictives nocives, une plus grande transparence sur les algorithmes qui suggèrent du contenu, et l’introduction d’un « mode jeunesse » limitant la publicité ciblée et les designs addictifs pour les enfants.

**Impact** – Cet effort est important car il rend les entreprises responsables de la protection des jeunes utilisateurs. Par exemple, le rapport demande des règles claires contre la transformation des enfants en influenceurs à des fins commerciales et souhaite un code de conduite pour le marketing d’influence. Il aborde aussi les nouveaux défis tels que les contenus générés par l’IA et la manipulation émotionnelle par des compagnons virtuels d’IA. Ces mesures visent à rendre le monde en ligne plus sûr et plus compréhensible pour les enfants.

**Prochaine étape** – Le rapport sera soumis au vote du Parlement européen complet en septembre. S’il est approuvé, il pourrait conduire à des lois plus strictes et à de meilleurs outils de protection pour les enfants dans tous les pays de l’UE. La Commission européenne et les États membres travailleront également sur des règles cohérentes concernant la vie privée, les services en ligne et l’utilisation de l’IA.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le Parlement européen fait avancer des règles claires pour rendre les réseaux sociaux plus sûrs et plus équitables pour les enfants en limitant les contenus nuisibles, en augmentant la transparence et en protégeant les mineurs contre l’exploitation.
articles.personality_title_es: El Parlamento Europeo impulsa reglas más estrictas para proteger a los niños en las redes sociales
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Millones de niños y jóvenes en la Unión Europea pasan varias horas al día en redes sociales e internet. Estudios muestran que pasar demasiado tiempo en línea, especialmente en redes sociales, puede afectar negativamente su salud mental y bienestar.

**Qué pasó** – El martes, el Comité de Cultura y Educación del Parlamento Europeo apoyó una mejor aplicación de las normas que protegen a los menores en línea. Quieren que las plataformas sociales diseñen sus servicios pensando en la seguridad desde el principio. Esto incluye prohibir funciones adictivas dañinas, aumentar la transparencia sobre cómo los algoritmos recomiendan contenido, e introducir un “modo joven” que limite la publicidad dirigida y el diseño adictivo para los niños.

**Impacto** – Este esfuerzo es importante porque responsabiliza a las empresas de redes sociales de proteger a los usuarios jóvenes. Por ejemplo, el informe pide reglas claras para evitar que los niños se conviertan en influencers con fines comerciales y busca un código de conducta para el marketing de influencers. También aborda nuevos retos como el contenido falso generado por IA y la manipulación emocional por compañeros virtuales de IA. Estas medidas buscan hacer el mundo en línea más seguro y fácil de entender para los niños.

**Próximo paso** – El informe será votado por el pleno del Parlamento Europeo en septiembre. Si se aprueba, podría llevar a leyes más estrictas y mejores herramientas de protección para niños en todos los países de la UE. La Comisión Europea y los estados miembros también trabajarán en normas coherentes sobre privacidad, servicios en línea y uso de IA.

**Resumen en una frase** – El Parlamento Europeo avanza en reglas claras para hacer las redes sociales más seguras y justas para los niños, limitando contenidos dañinos, aumentando la transparencia y protegiendo a los menores de la explotación.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a gentle guardian owl perched protectively over a glowing smartphone screen displaying softly illuminated, simplified childlike silhouettes surrounded by transparent, shield-like bubbles symbolizing privacy and safety, set against a calm, natural-toned background that evokes a secure digital environment crafted with care.

articles.title: People continue to stand by refugees, Gen Z shows most empathy
articles.summary: Protection for refugees has been enshrined in international law for more than seven decades, but how much support is there today for those fleeing conflict and persecution?
articles.content:
Why this matters

Most people still support the right to seek asylum

Resettlement numbers decrease massively

Misinformation has not eroded public compassion

The Refugee Convention marks 75 years of protection

Unveiling new data on Tuesday, the UN refugee agency, UNHCR, said that despite a growing volume of fake news and hate speech about asylum seekers, public support for refugees is stronger than public debate often suggests.

This support has remained broadly stable for years, “despite political tension, economic pressure, and as you all know very well, a very polarized debate”, said Dominique Hyde, UNHCR’s Director of External Relations.

Together with pollster Ipsos, the agency found that two in three people across 29 countries agreed that those fleeing war or persecution should be able to seek refuge in another country.

Double vision

At the same time, around the same number believe that many of those seeking protection do not really need it. “Many of these people are the same people; they hold both views at the same time,” said Trinh Tu, Managing Director of Ipsos UK.

“What we see at the moment is a tension between people wanting to support those in desperate need at the same time as having doubts about whether the system is working as it should,” in particular, asylum systems, border management and integration, Ms. Tu explained.

Generous to a fault

This perspective seems to prevail in the UK, where “we've got the lowest net migration in Britain, but at the same time, half of the population thinks that actually immigration has gone mad”, even though the facts show otherwise, she added.

In Germany and Sweden, where refugee intake is “quite sizeable”, UNHCR’s Ms. Hyde noted that support for asylum seekers remains relatively strong. In Türkiye and Poland, support is not as strong as in previous years.

I sat with mothers, I sat with fathers who had barely enough to eat

The veteran humanitarian official insisted on the need for continued international support for asylum seekers, citing the difficulties many countries face trying to host tens of thousands of people fleeing conflict. "Generosity cannot replace this international responsibility," Ms. Hyde stressed.

She described visiting Busuma camp in eastern Burundi, which shelters more than 57,000 Congolese refugees who have fled intense fighting in the Democratic Republic of Congo. Only around four in 10 people had somewhere to stay, despite the harsh conditions at 2,000 metres.

"I sat with mothers, I sat with fathers who had barely enough to eat," Ms. Hyde said. "I listened to families describe their overcrowded shelters, if they were lucky enough to have a shelter. I spoke to families about how unsafe they felt the water was. And not just unsafe, not sufficient. And nights spent, in exposed cold because it was at 2000 metres of altitude, and in the day, just sheer heat."

Boomers' worry

Of the more than 21,500 people surveyed, younger respondents are far more positive about refugees than Baby Boomers.

Almost half of Gen Z (born 1997 – 2012) believe that refugees will successfully integrate, compared with 39 per cent of Baby Boomers (1946 –1964).

“Gen Z respondents were less likely to support border closures or express doubts about refugees' motivations. Even so, concerns about integration, border management and the authenticity of asylum claims remained to some degree across age groups,” UNHCR said.

Supporting role

The UNHCR-Ipsos survey indicated that support for refugees is strongest in Sweden and the Netherlands (78 per cent), followed by Spain (76 per cent). Australia, Brazil and the United States expressed the most positive views on the benefits of refugee integration.

Some countries showed distinct shifts over time, including in Japan, where support for people seeking refuge rose to 64 per cent from 23 per cent in 2019, and in France, where it has climbed to 68 per cent from 43 per cent over the same period.

When asked about specific displacement situations and how they would prefer to respond, people prioritized direct emergency assistance, alongside diplomatic action and temporary protection.

The findings suggest that many people believe that refugee protection should include more alternatives than resettlement, although this remains a vital protection pathway for the most vulnerable refugees, 75 years after the Refugee Convention was adopted in Geneva.

“What we know is that many people support the right to seek safety while also questioning whether asylum systems are fair, efficient, and properly managed,” said Ms. Hyde. “This is an important message for this anniversary, 75 years on, the challenge is not only to defend the Convention, but also to make sure that the promise works.”

Rising needs, less support

In 2025, conflicts, violence and persecution continued to force millions of people from their homes, while climate shocks, economic instability and political fragility deepened needs.

UNHCR aimed to protect and assist 129.4 million people, close to 2024 levels. At the same time, available resources fell by $1.2 billion to $3.9 billion. UNHCR therefore had to respond to almost the same level of need with resources last seen in 2016, when the global population of forcibly displaced and stateless people was less than half its current size.

Resettlement challenge
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article reports on sustained and broad public support for refugee protection worldwide despite challenges such as misinformation and political tension. It highlights significant data from a UNHCR-Ipsos survey showing empathy especially among younger generations, and emphasizes the ongoing global commitment to uphold refugee rights 75 years after the Refugee Convention. The story has meaningful societal impact by reflecting positive public attitudes toward vulnerable displaced populations and the importance of international responsibility.
articles.personality_title: Global support for refugees remains strong, especially among young people
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – For more than 75 years, international law has protected the right of people to seek safety when fleeing war or danger. However, recent years have seen fewer refugees being resettled, and many worry about the fairness of asylum systems.

**What happened** – The United Nations refugee agency, UNHCR, shared new data from a survey done with Ipsos across 29 countries. It found that about two-thirds of people believe refugees should be allowed to find safety in another country. Despite some doubts about how the system works, public support for refugees has stayed steady, even with the spread of false information and political debates.

**Impact** – This shows that many people still care about refugees and want to help, especially younger generations like Gen Z, who are more positive about refugees fitting into new communities. For example, nearly half of Gen Z believe refugees will successfully integrate, compared to fewer older adults. Countries like Sweden and the Netherlands show the highest support. This support matters because millions of people continue to be forced from their homes, but resources to help them have decreased.

**What's next step** – UNHCR and governments need to keep protecting refugees and improving asylum systems so they work better and are fairer. Public support can help encourage leaders to provide enough resources and find new ways to help refugees beyond just resettlement.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Despite challenges, many people worldwide, especially young ones, continue to support refugees’ right to safety and a chance to rebuild their lives.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le soutien mondial aux réfugiés reste fort, surtout chez les jeunes
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Depuis plus de 75 ans, le droit international protège le droit des personnes à chercher refuge en cas de guerre ou de danger. Pourtant, le nombre de réfugiés réinstallés a diminué ces dernières années, et beaucoup s'inquiètent de l'équité des systèmes d'asile.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – L'agence des Nations Unies pour les réfugiés, le HCR, a publié de nouvelles données issues d'un sondage réalisé avec Ipsos dans 29 pays. Elles montrent qu'environ deux tiers des personnes pensent que les réfugiés devraient pouvoir trouver refuge dans un autre pays. Malgré certains doutes sur le fonctionnement du système, le soutien public est resté stable, malgré la désinformation et les débats politiques.

**Impact** – Cela montre que beaucoup de gens se soucient encore des réfugiés et veulent les aider, surtout les jeunes générations comme la Génération Z, plus optimistes sur l'intégration des réfugiés. Par exemple, près de la moitié des jeunes pensent que les réfugiés réussiront à s'intégrer, contre moins d'adultes plus âgés. Des pays comme la Suède et les Pays-Bas affichent le plus grand soutien. Ce soutien est important car des millions de personnes continuent d'être forcées de fuir, alors que les ressources pour les aider ont diminué.

**Prochaine étape** – Le HCR et les gouvernements doivent continuer à protéger les réfugiés et améliorer les systèmes d'asile pour qu'ils soient plus justes et efficaces. Le soutien public peut encourager les dirigeants à fournir suffisamment de ressources et à trouver de nouvelles façons d'aider les réfugiés au-delà de la réinstallation.

**Une phrase clé** – Malgré les défis, beaucoup de personnes dans le monde, surtout les jeunes, continuent de soutenir le droit des réfugiés à la sécurité et à une nouvelle vie.
articles.personality_title_es: El apoyo global a los refugiados sigue fuerte, especialmente entre los jóvenes
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante más de 75 años, el derecho internacional ha protegido el derecho de las personas a buscar seguridad cuando huyen de la guerra o el peligro. Sin embargo, en los últimos años ha disminuido el número de refugiados reasentados y hay preocupaciones sobre la justicia de los sistemas de asilo.

**Qué pasó** – La agencia de refugiados de la ONU, ACNUR, compartió nuevos datos de una encuesta realizada con Ipsos en 29 países. Encontraron que alrededor de dos tercios de las personas creen que los refugiados deberían poder buscar seguridad en otro país. A pesar de algunas dudas sobre cómo funciona el sistema, el apoyo público a los refugiados se ha mantenido estable, incluso con la difusión de información falsa y debates políticos.

**Impacto** – Esto muestra que muchas personas todavía se preocupan por los refugiados y quieren ayudar, especialmente las generaciones jóvenes como la Generación Z, que son más positivas sobre la integración de los refugiados. Por ejemplo, casi la mitad de la Generación Z cree que los refugiados se integrarán con éxito, en comparación con menos adultos mayores. Países como Suecia y los Países Bajos muestran el mayor apoyo. Este apoyo es importante porque millones de personas siguen siendo desplazadas, pero los recursos para ayudarlas han disminuido.

**Próximo paso** – ACNUR y los gobiernos deben seguir protegiendo a los refugiados y mejorar los sistemas de asilo para que funcionen mejor y sean más justos. El apoyo público puede ayudar a que los líderes proporcionen suficientes recursos y encuentren nuevas formas de ayudar a los refugiados más allá del reasentamiento.

**Conclusión en una frase** – A pesar de los desafíos, muchas personas en todo el mundo, especialmente los jóvenes, continúan apoyando el derecho de los refugiados a la seguridad y a reconstruir sus vidas.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of diverse, gently glowing paper boats floating together on a calm river at sunset, symbolizing refugees’ journeys and public support, with softly silhouetted hands reaching out from the riverbanks in gestures of empathy and protection, all rendered in natural, muted colors.

articles.title: ‘I have never had the opportunity to vote’: How one Haitian doctor’s initiative is getting people involved in civic life
articles.summary: Haiti’s daily hardships have made Dr. Lucna Henrisme focus on teaching youth and women about their rights and duties as citizens, so that they can get involved in civic life.
articles.content:
Dr. Lucna Henrisme is a 30-year-old medical doctor, International Visitor Leadership Program (IVLP) fellow and passionate pro-democracy activist, but with Haiti facing a crisis marked by insecurity and gang violence, she refuses to limit her work to the medical field. Living through Haiti’s daily hardships has made her focus on a critical mission: teaching people about their rights and duties as citizens, with a particular focus on helping young people and women get involved and take an active part in politics.

Few young people are involved in local politics in Haiti. This is because many are disappointed by promises of change that never happen. Every new government maintains the same system based on corruption, nepotism, and favouritism, and nothing really changes for the population. Another reason is the violence and intimidation in the political system. There is also a lack of civic education; many Haitians do not know their rights and duties because there is no basic civic training, especially for those who did not have access to education.

In an effort to reshape this trend, Dr. Henrisme founded an organisation called Mouvman SÈVI (The SERVICE Movement) in June 2024, through which she organises awareness activities, workshops, public meetings and discussion sessions across the country. Its main goal? To encourage young people and women to enter politics, SEVI is providing civic education so they can learn about their rights and duties. It also aims to encourage interest in active politics so they can seek political positions, participate in decisions being made for the country, and ensure their voices are included when important decisions are being made.

In an interview with Global Voices via a Messenger call, Dr. Henrisme said Haiti’s ongoing political crisis has left her disappointed, as well as concerned about the resulting challenges for democracy. Following the assassination of then-President Jovenel Moïse on July 7, 2021, Haiti has remained in a continuous state of transition. The country has been governed by three successive transitional administrations, starting with former Prime Minister Ariel Henry, followed by the Transitional Presidential Council (CPT) and most recently, under the leadership of former Prime Minister Garry Conille and current Prime Minister Alix Didier Fils-Aimé. While the main responsibilities of these transitional governments were to improve security and hold elections, neither goal has yet been reached.

The chances of elections being held in Haiti in 2026 are still unclear because security problems on the ground persist. Criminal gangs still control many areas, primarily in the West Department where the capital, Port-au-Prince, is located, but also in large portions of the Artibonite and Centre Departments. Continuing gang violence has forced over 1.5 million people to leave their homes and live in very difficult conditions in makeshift camps.

Throughout it all, Dr. Henrisme has remained consistent in her strong support of respect for the Constitution. She has been urging the current transitional government to deliver fair elections by honouring the governance pact, called the National Pact for Stability and the Organization of Elections, which a majority of national political parties signed.

Haiti has not held general elections since 2016, and parliament has not been functioning since January 2020, essentially rendering all national and local officials transitional. Dr. Henrisme believes that this situation limits democracy in Haiti, especially for young people who cannot exercise their civic rights:

Jenerasyon pa m nan pa janm viv sa yo rele demokrasi, ni vote nan yon eleksyon. Sa se yon bagay ki ban nou anpil pwoblèm kòm jèn kap viv nan yon peyi kote mwen paka egzèse dwa politik mwen nan eleksyon. Paske, depi mwen gen laj pou mwen patisipe nan pran desizyon politik, mwen pa janm jwenn opòtinite pou mwen vote, paske eleksyon pa janm òganize.

My generation has never experienced what is called democracy, nor have we voted in an election. This is a big problem for us as young people living in a country where I cannot exercise my political rights in an election. Because, since I reached the age to participate in political decision-making, I have never had the opportunity to vote, because elections are never organised.

Issuing a direct appeal to the authorities, she added:

Fòk gouvènman an aplike pak gouvènabilite li te siyen ak pati politik yo, pou yo ka òganize eleksyon kredib ak transparan pou retabli lejitimite demokratik jan pak la prevwa li.

The government must implement the governance pact it signed with political parties, in order to organise fair elections that bring back democratic legitimacy and restore trust in elected leaders, just as the pact intended.

Dr. Henrisme believes the current crisis can only be solved if local leaders shift their focus to consider the everyday realities of people’s lives. Emphasising that rebuilding trust between the government and its citizens is necessary for future progress, she envisions the state, the Haitian people and foreign partners working together to help the country move forward:

Kriz Ayiti ap viv la kapab rezoud si aktè yo gen yon sèl objektif, ki se pèp ayisyen an. Mank konfyans sitwayen yo nan Leta pa pèmèt peyi a soti nan sitiyasyon li ye a. Fòk konfyans retabli ant Leta ak sitwayen yo pou gen chanjman.

The crisis Haiti is facing can be solved if the actors share a single objective: the Haitian people. The citizens’ lack of trust in the state prevents the country from escaping its current situation. Trust must be restored between the state and the citizens for real change to happen.

Despite opportunities to live and practice medicine abroad, Dr. Henrisme has chosen to remain in Haiti and help the people most in need. She believes in the power of her generation to change things for the better:

Jenerasyon pa nou an gen espwa. Se yon kriz ayisyen, li dwe rezoud ant ayisyen. Fòk jenn yo ak fanm yo fè pati solisyon peyi a.

Our generation has hope. This is a Haitian crisis, and it must be resolved by Haitians. Young people and women must be part of the country’s solution.

The realities on the ground remain deeply complex. Armed gangs continue to impose their rule across several regions, while the humanitarian crisis steadily worsens. Additionally, growing disagreements between the government and influential political parties regarding the resuscitation of the electoral process threaten to further prolong political uncertainty and instability in Haiti.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a Haitian doctor's proactive initiative to educate and empower youth and women about their civic rights and duties amid a severe political crisis. This effort promotes democratic participation and aims to restore trust and legitimacy in governance, addressing broad societal challenges with tangible community impact.
articles.personality_title: Haitian doctor empowers youth and women to engage in politics amid crisis
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Haiti has faced ongoing political instability, violence, and a lack of elections since 2016. Many young people and women feel disconnected from politics due to corruption, insecurity, and little civic education.

**What happened** – Dr. Lucna Henrisme, a 30-year-old Haitian doctor and activist, started an organization called Mouvman SÈVI (The SERVICE Movement) in June 2024. Through workshops and public meetings, she teaches young people and women about their rights and encourages them to participate in politics.

**Impact** – This initiative helps people understand their civic duties and offers a way to get involved despite the challenges in Haiti. Dr. Henrisme’s work promotes hope by giving youth and women the tools to demand fair elections and better governance, which has not happened in years.

**What's next step** – Dr. Henrisme calls on the government to follow through on a signed pact to hold credible elections. The movement aims to keep educating citizens and encouraging political participation to rebuild trust between the people and the state.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A Haitian doctor’s effort to educate and engage youth and women in politics offers a new path toward democratic participation during a time of crisis.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une médecin haïtienne encourage les jeunes et les femmes à s’impliquer en politique malgré la crise
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Haïti fait face à une instabilité politique, à la violence et à l’absence d’élections depuis 2016. Beaucoup de jeunes et de femmes se sentent éloignés de la politique à cause de la corruption, de l’insécurité et du manque d’éducation civique.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – La Dre Lucna Henrisme, médecin haïtienne de 30 ans et activiste, a créé en juin 2024 une organisation appelée Mouvman SÈVI (Le Mouvement SERVICE). Par des ateliers et des réunions publiques, elle enseigne aux jeunes et aux femmes leurs droits et les encourage à participer à la vie politique.

**Impact** – Cette initiative aide les gens à comprendre leurs devoirs civiques et à s’impliquer malgré les difficultés en Haïti. Le travail de la Dre Henrisme apporte de l’espoir en donnant aux jeunes et aux femmes les moyens de réclamer des élections justes et une meilleure gouvernance, absentes depuis plusieurs années.

**Prochaine étape** – La Dre Henrisme appelle le gouvernement à respecter un pacte signé pour organiser des élections crédibles. Le mouvement vise à continuer l’éducation civique et à encourager la participation politique pour restaurer la confiance entre le peuple et l’État.

**Résumé en une phrase** – L’engagement d’une médecin haïtienne pour éduquer et mobiliser les jeunes et les femmes en politique ouvre une nouvelle voie vers la démocratie en pleine crise.
articles.personality_title_es: Una doctora haitiana impulsa la participación política de jóvenes y mujeres en medio de la crisis
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Haití enfrenta inestabilidad política, violencia y ausencia de elecciones desde 2016. Muchos jóvenes y mujeres se sienten alejados de la política por la corrupción, la inseguridad y la falta de educación cívica.

**Qué pasó** – La doctora Lucna Henrisme, médica haitiana de 30 años y activista, creó en junio de 2024 una organización llamada Mouvman SÈVI (El Movimiento SERVICIO). A través de talleres y reuniones públicas, enseña a jóvenes y mujeres sobre sus derechos y los anima a participar en política.

**Impacto** – Esta iniciativa ayuda a las personas a entender sus deberes cívicos y a involucrarse a pesar de los desafíos en Haití. El trabajo de la doctora Henrisme ofrece esperanza al dar herramientas a jóvenes y mujeres para exigir elecciones justas y mejor gobernanza, que no se han celebrado en años.

**Próximo paso** – La doctora Henrisme pide al gobierno que cumpla un pacto firmado para organizar elecciones creíbles. El movimiento seguirá educando y fomentando la participación política para reconstruir la confianza entre el pueblo y el estado.

**Frase clave** – El esfuerzo de una doctora haitiana por educar y motivar a jóvenes y mujeres en política abre un nuevo camino hacia la participación democrática en tiempos de crisis.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a young woman’s silhouette standing confidently at the center of a vibrant Haitian village square, surrounded by diverse groups of women and youth gathered in attentive circles, with symbolic elements like raised hands holding ballots and open books representing civic education, all rendered in natural, earthy tones that evoke hope, unity, and empowerment amidst a backdrop of modest homes and distant mountains.

articles.title: Heating oil customers to get compensation after price hikes
articles.summary: The cost of heating oil spiked following the US-Israel war with Iran which forced up crude prices.
articles.content:
Heating oil customers who had their orders cancelled and prices raised when the US-Israel war with Iran broke out will get compensation, the competition watchdog has said.

Some 1,700 households were forced to "re-order at significantly higher prices or go without fuel" costing them up to £350, the Competition Markets Authority (CMA) said.

Some suppliers have agreed to compensate customers and the regulator is planning legal action against those who have so far refused to do so, it added.

The UK and Ireland Fuel Distribution Association (UKIFDA), which represents heating oil suppliers, said "there were a small number of cases found which require redress".

Wholesale oil prices jumped from around $70 a barrel at the start of Iran war in February to almost $120 a barrel by the end of March as the conflict disrupted the transportation and production of energy in the region.

UK heating oil prices also jumped around this time. The CMA said on Wednesday that "average retail prices were, at their peak, 92% higher".

The CMA's investigation into the heating oil market found the price increases after the Iran war largely reflected rising wholesale costs and suppliers have not profited materially from the crisis.

However, it concluded heating oil customers are not as well protected as those connected to the energy grid.

It has recommended new regulations over how prices are quoted and cancellations are handled as well as "better support for vulnerable consumers".

Chancellor Rachel Reeves said: "It is reassuring to know it is a competitive market but the lack of protection for these households does concern me so we will look very seriously at what can be done."

UKIFDA chief executive Ken Cronin said: "We will work with all government bodies on the recommendations set out in this report."

Meanwhile, the CMA has not said how many suppliers have agreed to compensate customers for cancelled orders, how many customers will receive a pay out, or how much they will get.

"Those who paid more to replace their cancelled order will receive a payment covering the difference, while those who did not buy replacement oil will have their original orders honoured at the agreed price," it said.

"[We are] preparing to take court-based enforcement action against firms that fail to compensate customers voluntarily," it added.

The BBC understands more details will be provided once the scheme is up and running.

The CMA's report on the heating oil sector follows a four-month investigation launched in March.

Those who use heating oil often store it in a tank outside their property and are among the first to feel the impact of rising prices.

Some 1.5 million households depend on heating oil, but do not have the same consumer protections as electricity and gas customers, according to the CMA.

Most of those are in Northern Ireland, where the watchdog says 60% of households rely on it.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant positive development where heating oil customers affected by price hikes and order cancellations due to geopolitical conflict will receive compensation. This action by the competition watchdog and supplier agreements provide tangible financial relief to vulnerable households, addressing a gap in consumer protection for a large population reliant on heating oil. The story has broad societal impact, clear substance, and meaningful real-world benefits.
articles.personality_title: Heating oil customers to receive compensation after price rises
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The price of heating oil rose sharply after the war between the US, Israel, and Iran began. This conflict caused crude oil prices to jump, which affected heating oil costs for many households.

**What happened** – About 1,700 households had their heating oil orders canceled or had to pay much higher prices. The UK’s Competition Markets Authority (CMA) said these customers will get compensation. Some suppliers agreed to pay back customers, and the CMA is ready to take legal action against those who refuse.

**Impact** – This is important because many people who use heating oil do not have the same protections as those connected to gas or electricity. The price increases were mostly due to higher wholesale costs, but customers faced unfair cancellations and price hikes. The compensation helps people who had to pay up to £350 more or risked running out of fuel.

**What’s next step** – The CMA plans to introduce new rules to make pricing clearer and prevent unfair order cancellations. They also want better support for vulnerable customers. The government and suppliers will work together to improve protections for those relying on heating oil.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Heating oil customers affected by sudden price rises and cancellations will be compensated, and new rules will help protect them in the future.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les clients de fioul domestique vont être indemnisés après la hausse des prix
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le prix du fioul domestique a fortement augmenté après le début de la guerre entre les États-Unis, Israël et l’Iran. Ce conflit a fait grimper les prix du pétrole brut, ce qui a impacté le coût du fioul pour de nombreux foyers.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Environ 1 700 foyers ont vu leurs commandes de fioul annulées ou ont dû payer des prix beaucoup plus élevés. L’Autorité de la concurrence britannique (CMA) a annoncé que ces clients seront indemnisés. Certains fournisseurs ont accepté de rembourser leurs clients, et la CMA est prête à engager des poursuites contre ceux qui refusent.

**Impact** – Cela est important car beaucoup de personnes utilisant du fioul ne bénéficient pas des mêmes protections que celles raccordées au gaz ou à l’électricité. La hausse des prix s’explique principalement par l’augmentation des coûts de gros, mais les clients ont subi des annulations injustes et des hausses de prix. L’indemnisation aide ceux qui ont dû payer jusqu’à 350 £ de plus ou risquaient de manquer de carburant.

**Prochaines étapes** – La CMA prévoit d’introduire de nouvelles règles pour rendre les prix plus clairs et empêcher les annulations injustifiées. Elle souhaite aussi un meilleur soutien pour les consommateurs vulnérables. Le gouvernement et les fournisseurs travailleront ensemble pour renforcer la protection des utilisateurs de fioul.

**Phrase clé** – Les clients de fioul touchés par les hausses soudaines de prix et les annulations seront indemnisés, et de nouvelles règles amélioreront leur protection à l’avenir.
articles.personality_title_es: Los clientes de combustible para calefacción recibirán compensación tras subidas de precio
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El precio del combustible para calefacción subió mucho después de que comenzara la guerra entre Estados Unidos, Israel e Irán. Este conflicto hizo que los precios del petróleo crudo aumentaran, lo que afectó el costo del combustible para muchos hogares.

**Qué pasó** – Unos 1,700 hogares tuvieron sus pedidos cancelados o tuvieron que pagar precios mucho más altos. La Autoridad de Mercados y Competencia del Reino Unido (CMA) dijo que estos clientes recibirán una compensación. Algunos proveedores aceptaron pagar a sus clientes, y la CMA está lista para tomar acciones legales contra quienes se nieguen.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque muchas personas que usan combustible para calefacción no tienen las mismas protecciones que quienes están conectados al gas o la electricidad. El aumento de precios se debió principalmente a mayores costos al por mayor, pero los clientes enfrentaron cancelaciones injustas y subidas de precio. La compensación ayuda a quienes tuvieron que pagar hasta £350 más o corrieron el riesgo de quedarse sin combustible.

**Próximo paso** – La CMA planea introducir nuevas reglas para que los precios sean más claros y para evitar cancelaciones injustas. También quiere mejorar el apoyo a los clientes vulnerables. El gobierno y los proveedores trabajarán juntos para proteger mejor a quienes dependen del combustible para calefacción.

**Frase clave** – Los clientes de combustible para calefacción afectados por subidas repentinas de precio y cancelaciones recibirán compensación, y nuevas reglas mejorarán su protección en el futuro.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a cozy countryside home with a visible external heating oil tank gently glowing, surrounded by soft, natural hues of green and brown; nearby, a calm river shaped subtly like a rising graph symbolizes fluctuating fuel prices, while abstract golden scales balance a small pile of oil barrels on one side and a cluster of happy, simplified house silhouettes on the other, representing fair compensation and protection for heating oil customers.
